lesson.menu_desc: Indirect and embedded questions + cross-examination vocabulary -- 29 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 11 -- Cross-Examination: asking the hard questions in court.
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Luiz Bressane | Aula 11 -- Cross-Examination

listenings.text: Good morning. Before the witness returns, let me remind counsel how cross-examination works in this court. You may not ask leading questions of your own witness, but on cross-examination a leading question is allowed. When you want information, ask indirectly and politely: could you tell me where you were that evening, or do you know whether the door was locked. When you challenge the witness, be direct, but never badger. If an answer is unclear, ask the witness to clarify rather than to argue. Probe one fact at a time. If you find an inconsistency between today's answer and the earlier statement, point it out calmly and let the court draw its own conclusion. That is how credibility is tested.

You are listening to the legal affairs review. In court today, the defense conducted a careful cross-examination of the key witness. Rather than attacking, the lawyer asked indirectly whether the witness had been certain about the time. The witness grew evasive, so counsel rephrased the question more simply. When asked if she could explain the gap in her account, she finally conceded that she had not been wearing her glasses. The lawyer did not badger her; he simply probed the inconsistency until her credibility was in doubt. Reporters were told that a ruling is expected on Thursday. We will follow the case as it develops.
